coredata.dc:title: Novel electrochemical sensor based on modified glassy carbon electrode with graphene quantum dots, chitosan and nickel molybdate nanocomposites for diazinon and optimal design by the Taguchi method
coredata.dc:creator: Ghiasi, Tayebeh
Ahmadi, Shahin
Ahmadi, Elahe
Bavil Olyai, Mohamad Reza Talei
Khodadadi, Zahra
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a new sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor was offered based on the modification of activated glassy carbon electrode (GCEox) with graphene quantum dots, chitosan, and nickel molybdate (NiMoO4) nanocomposites for diazinon determination. A simple method of fabricating a sensor was presented based on covalent immobilization of polymer and nanoparticle due to functional groups on the GCEox surface. The surface of the constructed sensor was characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Optimal factors of experiments have been evaluated and analyzed using Minitab software by the Taguchi method (TM). Under optimized conditions, the constructed sensor illustrated two linear ranges between 0.1 and 330 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 30 nM using differential pulse voltammetry methods. Analytical parameters were determined, and the results were compared with similarly reported data. The proposed sensor display reasonable stability, good reproducibility, selectivity to the detection of the diazinon. The effect of interferences was studied on the sensor's function, accompanied by the recovery analysis of diazinon in cucumber and tomato real samples.

head.ce:title: Novel electrochemical sensor based on modified glassy carbon electrode with graphene quantum dots, chitosan and nickel molybdate nanocomposites for diazinon and optimal design by the Taguchi method

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a new sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor was offered based on the modification of activated glassy carbon electrode (GCEox) with graphene quantum dots, chitosan, and nickel molybdate (NiMoO4) nanocomposites for diazinon determination. A simple method of fabricating a sensor was presented based on covalent immobilization of polymer and nanoparticle due to functional groups on the GCEox surface. The surface of the constructed sensor was characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Optimal factors of experiments have been evaluated and analyzed using Minitab software by the Taguchi method (TM). Under optimized conditions, the constructed sensor illustrated two linear ranges between 0.1 and 330 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 30 nM using differential pulse voltammetry methods. Analytical parameters were determined, and the results were compared with similarly reported data. The proposed sensor display reasonable stability, good reproducibility, selectivity to the detection of the diazinon. The effect of interferences was studied on the sensor's function, accompanied by the recovery analysis of diazinon in cucumber and tomato real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the increase in crop cultivation has resulted in the increased consumption of pesticides; therefore, exposure to these substances has expanded and has many side effects and unfavorable effects on human health. Environmental pollution due to pesticides has also increased a serious global concern. Exposure to pesticides has many detrimental effects, and each of them can have specific impacts on peoples, depend on their chemical structure. Organophosphorus compounds such as diazinon (DZ) are among the most widely used pesticides in agriculture and are considered environmental and health risk factors [1–3]. DZ cause acetylcholine (ACh) accumulation and, consequently, severe neural stimulation by inhibiting cholinesterase (ChE) function.

To study the harmful and complications of chronic DZ poisoning can be noted the nerve lesions such as peripheral polyneuropathy can lead to muscle weakness, tingling, and numbness on the hands and feet. Harmful effects on the gastrointestinal tract, skin, lungs, and breathing problems can also be mentioned [4]. Due to the increasing use of pesticides, the development of accurate and reliable analytical methods seems necessary for measuring these compounds in various samples such as water, soil, food, and biological samples. Therefore, the development and use of sensitive, cost-effective, easy, reliable, and accurate procedures can affect the applicability of measurements in this field.

In the past decades, different procedures have been used to measuring the amount of DZ in agricultural products, including gas chromatography (GC) [5,6], liquid chromatography (LC) [7,8], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9–11] and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [12]. Some of these procedures provide good detection limits and highly sensitive methods, but to use them, the steps of sample preparation and preconcentration are highly intricate. Therefore these methods are time-consuming and expensive and should be performed by equipment and highly trained personnel [13,14]. To overcome these problems, the use of possible procedures for the detection of pesticides in environmental and biological samples, such as electrochemical methods, has attracted many researchers' attention.

The use of these methods does not require complicated purification and preconcentration steps, and a simple sample preparation step can be employed to determine the amount of analyte [15]. The few electrochemistry studies on DZ have been performed [16–18]. However, some studies on this compound have been performed by modifying electrodes as sensors [19–23].

Among the modifiers, carbon-based nanomaterials such as GQD have been used for attractive and useful properties over the past few years. The GQDs (new zero-dimensional compounds) consists of the small layers of single-or more layers of graphene by thickness and diameter less than 30 and 20 nm, respectively [24]. Today, GQDs have attracted much attention in nanomaterials technology due to their essential properties, including large surface area (2630 m2/g), high thermal conductivity, low cytotoxicity, high fluorescence activity, chemical inertness, stability, high optical and catalytic properties, and excellent solubility of these compounds, especially in water [25–27]. These compounds also consisted of bonding surfaces with good chemical properties due to functional groups on their surface (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl) and their π-π interactions, which made them better replace than QDs [28].

CS is one of the most abundant amino polysaccharides in nature with unique structure and multipurpose properties. The most essential features that make it suitable for these applications could be biocompatibility, biodegradability, and non-toxicity. CS is selected as the inert matrix for the development and fabrication of sensors due to the ability to form the film, high water permeability, fast ion transfer, adhesion to hydrophilic surfaces, ease of chemical modifications, as well as the presence of amine and hydroxyl groups [29–31].

Metal molybdates, AMoO4-type compounds (where A is a divalent metal ion), have been attracted significant attention from researchers because of their electronic [32–34] and magnetic properties [35] and their many applications. For instance, they were successfully employed in selective oxidation reactions and are quite versatile catalysts for critical industrial processes [36–38]. Among them, NiMoO4 has been a subject of intensive research due to the many advantages such as high electrical conductivity, chemical stability, low cost [39], enhanced electrochemical performance [40], photoluminescence [41], magnetic [42], electronic [32,43], optical, photocatalytic properties [44] and also catalytic properties for oxidation reactions [40,42,45].

The utilization of NiMoO4 in the electrochemical field, significantly as new modifiers in the construction of electrochemical sensors, has been increased due to the increment of selectivity, sensitivity, stability, and reproducibility analysis [46–49].

The design of experiments (DoE) is a powerful multivariate statistical approach to obtain the maximum possible amount of information about a system with the minimum number of experimental runs. Conducting fewer runs based on DoE can help save time and money in process optimization and keep experiment costs low [50,51].

Taguchi method (TM) is a statistical design method to optimize the parameters offered by Taguchi and Konishi. The statisticians have confirmed the Taguchi efforts, especially in the progress of designs to acquiring alteration. An exact selection of method parameters and dissociation of them into control and noise factors are the success in access to desirable results. The choice of control parameters must be performed to removes or decreases the effects of noises. To achieve the optimum results in the process, the TM acts as a suitable method for assessing control factors. The acquired results of the TM experiments are then employed to examine the data and, therefore, the anticipation of the typicality of the designed method [52–55]. TM can reduce the number of experiments and costs of them, ability to examine discrete factors, determine the contribution of factors, ability to estimate results in optimal conditions, define error contribution, and the possibility to obtain optimal conditions for several answers simultaneously [52].

In this study, we employed the composition of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), chitosan (CS), and nickel molybdate nanocomposites (NiMoO4, NMO) as modifiers for the construction of DZ sensor (NMO/GQDs/CS/GCEox). For the first time, this research evaluated the effect of special experimental conditions on multi-performance characteristics in constructed electrochemical sensors using utility-based TM for DZ detection based on the design of experiments (DOE).

An Autolab system potentiostat–galvanostat (Autolab, model PGSTAT 302N, The Netherlands) using a NOVA (1.11) software was exerted to all of the electrochemical experiments at room temperature. A platinum wire and a silver-silver chloride (Ag/AgCl, saturated KCl) electrode were utilized as auxiliary and reference electrodes, respectively. The EIS with an FRA 32 impedance analysis module was exerted for electrode surface investigation. The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were performed at NMO/GQDs/CS/GCEox as working electrode. All pH measurements were performed by Metrohm pH-meter (model 780) that was equipped with a glass electrode during the experiments. In these experiments, an ultrasonic bath (Euronda, eurosonic® 4D) was utilized to disperse the particles inside the solution and its uniformity. The synthesized NPs for modification of electrode was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) model MIRA Ш (TESCAN Co., Czech).

The high purity of DZ was prepared from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). The citric acid (CA), chitosan (CS), ethylene glycol (C2H4 (OH)2), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), molybdenum(II) nitrate salt (Mo(NO3)2·6H2O), nickel nitrate salt (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), phosphoric, boric and acetic acids, alumina, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sulphuric acid (H2SO4) were obtained from Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany). The BR buffer solution was prepared from phosphoric, boric, and acetic acids (0.04 M), and for the adjustment of the pH, NaOH solution (0.2 M) was used. Another chemical and reagents consumed in the present work were acquired of analytical grade and applied without additional purification. All tests were accomplished at room temperature.

The general approach for the preparation of GQDs was based on an easy bottom-up method by direct pyrolysis of citric acid [28]. At first, for the preparation of GQDs, citric acid (2 g) was transferred to liquid by heating at 200 °C. After 30 min of heating, the color of melted citric acid changed from colorless to orange, which demonstrated the formation of GQDs. Then the prepared orange liquid was dropped into 100 ml of NaOH solution (10 mg/ml), so that solution was stirred very vigorously for a safe, efficient synthesis. Finally, the obtained solution was neutralized (pH 7) using NaOH solution, and the prepared GQDs solution was maintained at 4 °C.
Synthesis of NMO NPs was accomplished according to previously published research based on the citrate sol–gel procedure [40].

In the first step, citric acid and ethylene glycol were mixed with a molar ratio of 1.5 and dissolved in distilled water. The nickel nitrate salt was added to the solution in the proportion of one-quarter of citric acid, and the obtained solution was stirred at 60 °C until a clear solution was obtained. In the next step, a stoichiometric amount of the molybdenum (II) nitrate salt was added to the obtained mixture, and the resulting product was stirred at 60 °C for 24 h to convert a high viscose gel. Then the prepared sample was dried overnight in an oven at 120 °C to the production of the NMO NPs and calcined in air at 750 °C for 6 h.

At first, the GCE was polished with alumina slurry (0.05 µm) to a mirror-like surface was achieved, then washed with deionized water in an ultrasonic bath. Subsequently, the cleaned GCE was activated through 30 cycles of CV procedure in 0.5 M H2SO4 with the potential window between −1.0 and 2.0 V at the scan rate of 50 mV to prepare the GCEox. In this step, functional groups such as hydroxyl oxygen (O-) and also carbonyl oxygen (COO–) groups were introduced onto the GCE surface, which caused the negative charge on the electrode surface [56]. The activated electrode was then washed with deionized water and dipped into the 1 ml CS solution (0.5% in acetic acid) for 30 min. CS could be immobilized on the surface of GCEox, through the electrostatic attraction between ammonium groups of CS and functional groups of the electrode surface (carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) to prepare CS/GCEox. Next, 1 ml of GQDs suspension was dispersed in 1 ml solution containing 10 mM NHS and 10 mM EDC and sonicated for about 2 h to activate the terminated carboxylic groups of the GQDs. The CS/GCEox was then immersed in the GQDs solution for 2 h to form GQDs/CS/GCEox. The abundant presence of functional groups, especially carboxyl groups in the GQDs, provided strong covalence bonding between GQDs with amino groups of CS and created functional groups on the activated electrode surface [57]. Subsequently, 5 μl of prepared NMO NPs (2 mg/ml) was cast on the surface of the GQDs/CS/GCEox and was dried at room temperature. The constructed sensor was named as NMO/GQDs/CS/GCEox. Followed by each step, the prepared electrode washed with buffer solution to eliminate the excess of the physically and weakly adsorbed substances. Finally, the prepared sensor was removed from the solution and washed with double distilled water, and afterward, it was exposed to air-drying and kept at room temperature for further use.

In this paper, the Minitab software has been used to determines the optimal conditions using the TM. The TM employs the orthogonal arrays (OA) to study many parameters with a smaller number of runs. OAs impressively decrease experimental errors and enhances the output and reproducibility of experimental conditions. This can also reduce the cost and time, along with the tests when fractionated designs are employed. These arrays are selected from the total number of experiments according to their specificity in the factorial method. Taguchi does not claim that the optimum response certainly exists in the experiments chosen; rather, using the arrays calculations can determine optimal conditions and response under these optimal conditions. Finally, by repeating the experiment and confirming the reproducibility of the response in these conditions (confirms the experiment), its accuracy will be achieved [58]. It should be noted that up to a maximum of 7 and fewer parameters can be used. The TM contains the most suitable OA for specifying the parameters with the proper columns and illustrates the combinations of experiment conditions [52].

DOE experiments were performed for five factors, including pH, deposition potential (Dep. pot., mV), deposition time (Dep. time, s), potential step (Pot. step, mV), and amplitude modulation (Mod. Amp., mV) with 5 levels (L25). This means that five factors and five levels were selected for each factor, which illustrated the selected factors and levels in Table 1 .

The selection of an optimum amount for parameters can be decreased or diminished by factor errors, leading to an inappropriate response. After calculating S/N ratio at each factor level by software, the optimal level of each factor was obtained and utilized to determine signal by sensor.
This method can be identified and controlled all design parameters, and so eliminate the uncontrollable factors called noise parameters.

The mean (ANOM) analysis was applied for all the tests based on TM to determine the optimal levels. The superiority of this method was to decrease the number of experiments. For this propose, software should choose the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) calculation, which shows the extent of the distribution response. Since this paper aims to maximize the DZ detection signal, therefore to get a complete understanding of factor effects, the S/N was used. In this study, the bigger or larger S/N (SNL) is better was applied, which could be expressed as follows [59]:
SN L
= - 10
log
1 / n
1 /
yi
2
where ‘y’ and ‘n’ are the observed data and number of observations, respectively.

In these experiments, the interaction between factors was not considered, and assumed factors would be considered independently. After successfully performing the TM, the obtained data from experiments were investigated to estimate factor effects. Then, the optimal levels of the control factors selected and performance predicted. The protocol steps consisted of the calculation of S/N at each factor level, optimal levels detection, optimal factors combining, the mathematical prediction of S/N produced by sensor in optimal conditions and comparing it with the S/N ratio of an experimental signal of sensor.
If the acquired result is similar to the software prediction, it can be employed this set of optimal levels, and if there is too much difference, then the chosen factor must be varied.

For measurement of DZ in real samples, the fresh cucumber and tomato were purchased from a local market. Some cucumber and tomato were also sampled and prepared from the farm sprayed with diazinon. Specimens contaminated with pesticides and organic samples separately were chopped into tiny particles and homogenized by an electric mixing machine [60]. After that, each sample (2 g) was separately mixed with 10 ml of BR buffer solution (pH 7.0), and then solutions were sonicated in an ultrasonic bath for 2 h. The prepared mixed solutions of samples were then filtered through filter paper under vacuum to eliminate the suspended particles. The obtained solutions were diluted in the BR buffer solution to a total volume of 100 ml, and the DZ quantity of samples was distinguished based on the standard addition method. The prepared solutions were retained in a refrigerator at 4 °C before utilization [61].

The TEM image of synthesized GQDs and NMO NPs was shown in Fig. 1 for the morphology study of synthesized NPs. Fig. 1A shows a typical TEM image of a GQDs sample prepared by a direct pyrolysis method of citric acid, revealing a relatively uniform size (approximately 15 nm), consistent with previous studies[28].

The acquired TEM image of constructed NMO NPs shown in Fig. 1B indicates an identical structure for synthesized NMO NPs with the crystalline appearance, almost tetragonal, consistent with previous research [62]. This image also manifests that the spherical shape particles were agglomerated together, and mean particle size was gained between the 20–30 nm. Moreover, the EDX spectrum and typical elemental mapping images of the NMO particles are illustrated in Fig. S1. As can be seen, typical EDX elemental mapping of elements shows molybdenum, nickel, oxygen elements, and carbon contamination in the merely crystalline perovskite structure without the presence of infected phases.

To characterization of the prepared sensor in each step, the SEM method was employed. The surface morphology of the GCE, GCEox, CS/GCEox, GQDs/CS/GCEox, and NMO/GQDs/CS/GCEox was inspected by SEM (Fig. 2 a–e). Fig. 2a shows a featureless morphology with a smooth and homogeneous surface for the GCE. As shown in Fig. 2b, the GCEox presents a highly porous structure, which confirms that GCE's surface is well covered with functional groups containing highly reactive oxygen and oxidized carbon during the activation of the surface electrode [56]. In the next step of modification, the CS's immobilization has changed the electrode surface morphology (Fig. 2c). As well as in Fig. 2d, after immobilization of the GQDs on the CS/GCEox, a new layer was formed on the electrode surface as small spots, which corroborated an immobilization of GQDs with the high surface area. The existence of different spherical NPs illustrated the formation of homogeneous distribution of NMO NPs (Fig. 2e).

The EIS method is often used to study changes occurring between the electrode surface interface and the solution. This method offers more details about the interfacial properties of electrodes during the construction process. In EIS studies, to monitor the charge transport within the electrode's surface, the solution of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- as an electrochemical probe is mainly used. In EIS measurements, the semicircle diameter of impedance at low frequencies is equal to the electron transfer resistance (Ret), which controls the electron transfer kinetics of the redox probe on the electrode surface, which commonly illustrates the faradaic process at the interface of electrode/electrolyte. The straight line of the Nyquist plots appeared at lower frequencies, corresponds to the diffusion process of the electroactive species in the solution to the electrode interface. The Nyquist plots at the GCE (curve a), GCEox (curve b), CS/GCEox (curve c), GQDs/CS/GCEox (curve d), and NMO/GQDs/CS/GCEox (curve e) were recorded at frequencies ranging from 0.01 Hz to 10 kHz in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−as shown in Fig. 3 A. The GCEox illustrated a straight line, which confirmed the electrode surface variation and created the electron transfer easier. This result can be obtained due to the large surface area in the GCEox, which could effectively increase the rate electron transfer between the electrode surface and [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and so decrease interface electron transfer resistance. After modifying GCEox with CS film, the Ret was increased, indicating evidence for immobilization of CS with the organic structure on the GCEox (curve c). After immobilization of GQDs on the surface of CS/GCEox (curve d), the Ret was significantly decreased compared with the CS/GCEox, which demonstrated GQDs have a significantly higher electrical conductivity than CS film [63]. The considerable increase in Ret at the surface of GQDs/CS/GCEox compared with the GCE surface can also be ascribed to the high repulsion between redox anion charges and sensor surface due to the presence of carboxyl groups of GQDs. Therefore, the electron transfer kinetics will be more difficult at the surface of the electrode [64]. After the final step, the immobilization of nanoparticle, semicircle, and electron transfer resistance was decreased due to the high conductivity of NiMoO4 [39].

The CV method was also employed to further characterize each step during GCE modification (Fig. 3B). For bare and modified GC electrodes, the peak currents of the redox probe indicated the presence of modifiers layers at the electrode surface and so different interactions between the probe in solution and modifiers at the surface. The recorded CVs of different electrodes agree well with those of EIS signals.

The effect of pH on the electrochemical behavior of 10 μM DZ was evaluated using the DPVs method in BR solution with the pH range 2–9. As shown in this Fig. 4 A, the cathodic peak current of DZ with the enhancement of pHs up to 3.0 was increased and then was decreased up to 9.0. Therefore, pH 3 was selected as optimum pH for further investigation, as shown in Fig. 4B. Furthermore, the linear relation between the pH and the reduction peak potential of DZ depicted in Fig. 4C, which illustrated by the enhancement of pH, the cathodic peak potential was shifted to negative extents. This trend has suggested the H+ participating in the electrochemical reduction process of DZ. The linear variation of reduction peak potential with pH (Fig. 4C) and the slope value of this plot (-68 mV per pH unit) indicated an equal number of electrons and protons in the electrochemical reduction of DZ on the surface of the NMO/GQDs/CS/GCEox. The electrochemical studies already revealed a two-electron and two-proton transfers process, which confirmed this study [16-18].

Fig. 5 shows the CVs of DZ at the surfaces of GCE (curve b), GCEox (curve c), CS/GCEox (curve d), GQDs/CS/GCEox (curve e), and NMO/GQDs/CS/GCEox (curve f) in BR buffer solution (pH 3). For a more detailed study of the sensor behavior, the CV of BR was obtained in the absence of DZ (curve a). As can be seen, the signal of DZ at the bare GCE is weakly enough, so that only a feeble reduction peak is observed at −0.88 V (curve b). The reduction current at the GCEox has increased significantly (curve c). This increase may be due to the morphology and electron transport property of the GCEox. In the next step, after modifying of the electrode with CS film, the signal of DZ slightly reduced (curve d), which can be described as the low efficiency of electron conductivity of the CS film and so the electron transfer process at the surface of this electrode was limited [65]. The analyte signal at the surface of GQDs/CS/GCEox (curve e) depicted a higher reduction current, which illustrated the GQDs/CS composite film has a large effective surface area than CS and GCEox. Also, decreasing the cathodic overpotential (-0.80 V) during the reduction process of DZ at the GQDs/CS/GCEox than the GCE, demonstrated the kinetics of electron transfer DZ was better considerably at this modified electrode.

Furthermore, the current signal at the NMO/GQDs/CS/GCEox surface (curve f) is greater than that obtained at the other electrodes. It can be related to the increased active surface area and the accumulation of DZ on the modified electrode surface. Moreover, a combination of NMO with GQDs provided many functional groups (N–H and COOH) on the inner-cavity surface. The outer surface of the modified electrode might create a particularly favorable reaction receptacle for the electroreduction of the analyte. In the cyclic voltammograms of DZ at the surface of all tested electrodes were not detected any oxidation peak during the reverse scan, which indicated the irreversible behavior for the electrochemical process of DZ.

The electrochemical response of 10 µM DZ on the prepared sensor in BR buffer solution (pH 3) at various scan rates from 10 to 300 mVs−1 was investigated (Fig. 6 A). Based on Fig. 6B, the cathodic peak currents were increased by enhancing the sweep rates, which illustrated the adsorption controlled process. This result can be investigated and confirmed by plotting the log Ip against log υ (Fig. 6C). A linear relationship between these two variables and the slope value of 0.6891 was demonstrated an adsorption controlled electrode process [66]. Also, this variation is accompanied by shifting the peak potential to more negative potentials. This trend of the DZ electrochemical behavior confirmed that reduction obeyed from an irreversible attribute.

The linear variation of the cathodic peak potentials of DZ with the logarithm of the scan rate illustrated in Fig. 6D, which could be used for calculation of the electron transfer rate constant (ks) and transfer coefficient (α) based on the Laviron theory [67]. For an irreversible electrode process with the slope of this plot (equal to − 2.303RT/αnF) could be evaluated the value of α based on the following equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: TM for the optimization of the experimental parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The responses in Table 3 for each level of each parameter were depicted based on the average of each signal (means of S/N ratios). Therefore, the obtained results for S/N ratios illustrated the significant effect of parameters on the signal variation. In this method, to decrease the variation in the measurement system, the S/N ratios should be maximized and decreased the standard deviation (SD). According to these data, the pH = 3.75, Dep. pot. = -150 mV, Dep. time = 160 s, Amp. Mod. = 80 mV and Pot. Step = -14 mV has been chosen as the optimal analysis conditions (Fig. 7 ). Finally, these levels of factors created the higher S/N ratios with lower SDs. Therefore, TM's acquired optimal parameters in theoretical results have confirmed the optimal conditions in experimental results. The experimental results in optimal conditions confirm the results of software prediction in the optimal conditions.

Also, responses were investigated by the 3D surface plots, as shown in Fig. 8 . Interaction between factors is thoroughly usual in experiments. Therefore, the effect of selected level factors on response was investigated and is illustrated in Fig. 8. The response surface plots can be utilized to recognize the significant interactions between the trial factors [68].

The response of surface plots was drawn by the regression model to illustrate the composed influences of Ip between the pH and step pot., pH, and Dep. Pot., pH, and Amp. Mod., pH and Dep. time, step pot. and Dep. pot., step pot. and Amp. Mod., step pot. and Dep. time, Dep. potential and Amp. Mod., Dep. pot. and Dep. time, Amp. Mod. and Dep. time, as shown in Fig. 8 with a symbol of a to j, respectively. As it is seen, the maximum Ip was obtained precisely based on the signals in the main effect plot (Fig. 7), which corroborated the interaction effects between the factors.

Hence, by looking at the effects of each factor separately, the overall moderate interactions between the factors on response using the sensor can be distinguished. It depicted there is a specified relation among the factors on signal evaluation. Three-dimensional surface plots for each pair factors were generated to estimate the effect of the combinations of the independent factors on the peak current. From the results of Fig. 8, it can be expressed the all factors are affecting parameters in this analysis and have the same and significant contribution share on signal values. Therefore, optimization based on the DOE provided a high model optimization closer to experimental signal values [69].

For acquiring the results to detection, the optimum levels of parameters, analysis of the experimental data by ANOVA test, and effects of parameters perform from validation experiments. The ANOVA test in Taguchi analysis is usually applied to characterize the effective factors on the average response and S/N ratio. Degree of freedom (DF), Sum of squares (SS), mean square (MS), F-value (F), and p-value (P) based on S/N data were offered in Table 4 . The sequential (Seq) and adjusted (Adj) sum of squares in the ANOVA shows the relative importance of each parameter so that the parameter with the higher SS has the greatest effect. The SS for the parameters in Table 4 illustrated that the Mod. Amp. factor has a higher SS value followed by Dep. time, Pot. Step, pH, and Dep. Pot. factors, respectively. Moreover, the p-values depicted the Mod. With the lowest p-value (0.022), Amp has considerable effect in this procedure at the 0.05 α-level, followed by Pot. Step, pH, Dep. time, and Dep. Pot. with higher p-values of 0.033, 0.055, 0.067 and 0.179, respectively. However, these parameters still have impressive effects in this procedure with their low standard deviations at the 0.05 α-level. Based on the F test, it can be concluded the all the parameters considered in the DOE have statistically impressive effects at 95% confidence limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reputability, reproducibility, and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reputability, reproducibility, and stability of the offered sensor were evaluated using DPV procedure. The repetitive DPVs of 10 μM of DZ in BR buffer solution (pH 3) for about 6 times showed a 1.6% decrease in the peak currents, which depicts the repeatability of this sensor. The reproducibility test of this sensor also was performed by the construction of four individual electrodes and then the measurement of DZ solution with a known concentration by each electrode. The results illustrated a standard deviation of less than ± 4% for responses. The constructed sensor also retained 96% of its primary activity after 30 days, which shows long-term stability. The recovery of DZ solution with different concentrations was calculated to be above 94%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 2.7%. Therefore, these results illustrated the offered senor has excellent stability, acceptable reproducibility, and repeatability.

The effects of some possible interferences, including organic and inorganic compounds on the sensor response, under optimal conditions, have been studied to evaluate the selectivity of the offered sensor and analytical application of the proposed method for the detection of DZ (10 μM).

Based on the acquired data, the following compounds up to concentration mentioned did not change the DZ current signal by 5%, and therefore depicted the high selectivity of the offered sensor (Table 6 ).

DZ concentrations were measured in cucumber and tomato samples to evaluate the applicability of the offered sensor in real samples. After sample preparation, as explained in section 2.7, the sensor was applied to the DZ determination by the DPV technique based on the standard addition method, and the results were summarized in table 7 . The results in Table 7 showed the acceptable recoveries of the spiked samples. Thus the offered sensor could be efficiently employed for DZ determination in real samples with various matrices.

This study presents a combination of different nanomaterials with distinct roles for the fabrication and widespread application of modified electrodes in electroanalytical chemistry. Indeed, a combination of independent properties of NMO, GQDs, and CS nanomaterials, led to the eminence of the designed sensor for DZ detection. Therefore, due to easy, fast preparation and relatively inexpensive, this sensor can be used as an excellent and useful tool for designing an electrochemical sensor for detection and analysis of DZ in real samples.

The NMO/GQDs/CS/GCEox for precise and swift analysis of DZ in cucumber and tomato as real samples has been effectively used as a sensitive voltammetric sensor. The constructed sensor illustrated good selectivity, sensitivity, and reproducibility. The applicability of the offered sensor was compared with other reported sensors for DZ measurement. The results were presented in Table 5, which depicts some of the analytical parameters of this sensor compared to other studies.

Finally, the constructed sensor was used for DZ detection with acceptable results in real samples. This study also presented the effectiveness and feasibility of using TM as a statistical experimental design to optimize variables for designing the electrochemical sensor to DZ measurement using the DPV method. TM's data analysis was applied to the study of five experimental variables at five levels (L25 orthogonal array). As well as, ANOVA analysis was used to investigate the relative significance of the effect of several parameters and was presented; all parameters have a considerable impact on the signal.
